quotes.text: In early times the "book of remembrance" was one in which inspired writings were recorded. See Moses 6:5, 6. In Persian times those who rendered service to the king had their names entered in a book with a notice of their merits, so that they might be rewarded at a future time. See Esther 6:1, 2. In the light of this custom the metaphor is readily understood.
quotes.speaker: Sidney B. Sperry
quotes.attr: Sidney B. Sperry, The Voice of Israel's Prophets, p.445

notes: Now Haman was come into the outward court--This was early in the morning. It is the invariable custom for kings in Eastern countries to transact business before the sun is hot, often in the open air, and so Haman was in all probability come officially to attend on his master.

What shall be done unto the man whom the king delighteth to honour?--In bestowing tokens of their favor, the kings of Persia do not at once, and as it were by their own will, determine the kind of honor that shall be awarded; but they turn to the courtier standing next in rank to themselves, and ask him what shall be done to the individual who has rendered the service specified; and according to the answer received, the royal mandate is issued.

the horse that the king rideth upon--Persia was a country of horses, and the highbred charger that the king rode upon acquired, in the eyes of his venal subjects, a sort of sacredness from that circumstance.
the royal apparel . . . which the king useth to wear--A coat which has been on the back of a king or prince is reckoned a most honorable gift, and is given with great ceremony.
and the crown royal which is set upon his head--either the royal turban, or it may be a tiara, with which, on state processions, the horse's head was adorned.

"Beaming with self-assurance Haman enters into the king's presence… Haman knows his glory is now really beginning to take shape. So, when the king asks him what should be done to a man who has won the king's favor he simply cannot think that the king is referring to him personally… Undoubtedly, in that case the king would have him in mind… So he gives a list of all the things he craves for himself … royal apparel, the royal crown… At that very moment falls the great blow, the stunning blow, the utterly incredible. The king says do all of this for Mordecai" (Friedrich Weinreb, Chance the Hidden Lord - The Amazing Scroll of Esther, 145-146).
7:1-6. ESTHER PLEADS FOR HER OWN LIFE AND THE LIFE OF HER PEOPLE
